subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly geographical and directly linked to IB Option D: Geophysical Hazards, enabling testable hypotheses.
Precise identification of EscazÃº and San Rafael with clear feasibility for primary data collection, supported by site tables.
weaknesses: Multiple mapping conventions missing (north arrows, scale bars, legends) across key figures.
Inconsistent toponymy (âSan Pabloâ vs âSan Rafaelâ) and occasional descriptive rather than analytical context.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student formulates an excellent, theory-linked research question and identifies precise field sites, persistent mapping convention errors and minor context inconsistencies limit the holistic achievement to the mid-range. Thus, Criterion A is awarded 2/3.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods (questionnaires, Spearmanâs rank) are appropriately described and linked to each hypothesis.
Correct implementation of convenience sampling with a substantial sample size (n = 240) and multilingual format.
weaknesses: Critical discussion of sampling bias and blank entries in the survey log is missing.
The theoretical rationale for choosing each statistical test is only briefly addressed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The methods are clearly described and relevant, and data collection is executed correctly. However, the absence of critical reflection on sampling bias and incomplete survey logs restrains the work to a solid middle band. Criterion B receives 2/3.

strengths: Primary datasets (income, education, age, preparedness scores) are largely relevant to the hypotheses.
Use of varied presentation techniques (bar charts, pie charts, tables) effectively contrasts distributions.
weaknesses: Axis labels, units and titles are occasionally unclear or missing.
Frequent convention errors in figures (no north arrows, poor colour keys, inconsistent coding schemes).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data are sufficient for analysis and mostly well presented, but occasional unclear labels and repeated convention errors prevent full marks. The work fits the mid-band for Quality and Treatment. Criterion C is scored 3/6.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Appropriate use of Spearmanâs rank correlations alongside descriptive statistics.
Identification of trends and anomalies linked back to hypotheses.
weaknesses: No statistical significance values or confidence levels are reported.
Analysis links to geophysical-hazard theory are only cursory and some data gaps are unaddressed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student correctly applies descriptive and basic statistical techniques and links results to hypotheses, the absence of significance testing and limited theoretical integration restrict the analysis to the lower end of the 3â4 band. Criterion D is awarded 3/8.

strengths: The conclusion summarises which hypotheses are accepted or rejected.
weaknesses: Lacks explicit reference to statistical thresholds or margin-of-error to fully support the conclusion.
Remains largely narrative without integration of analysis rigor.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides a clear statement of outcomes but fails to substantiate claims with statistical context. This places the conclusion in the lower band. Criterion E receives 1/2.

strengths: Identifies relevant strengths (e.g., sample size reliability, bilingual questionnaire) and weaknesses (convenience bias, timing issues).
Outlines improvement suggestions (alternative survey windows, use of software) with some mention of potential impact.
weaknesses: Suggestions are not elaborated with detailed rationale or anticipated effects.
Depth of evaluation is limited by brief explanations without examples of implementation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation appropriately outlines strengths/weaknesses and offers relevant improvement ideas, but lacks the comprehensive depth and impact analysis required for full marks. Criterion F is scored 2/3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Locator and site maps are present, but conventions are inconsistently applied: one hand-drawn tectonic map lacks scale accuracy, some Datawrapper maps omit north arrows or scale bars, and colour keys are hard to read.  Hence mapping is adequate but not fully meeting best-practice conventions.

The research question is clearly geographical, focused on earthquake preparedness, and is explicitly tied to IB Option D: Geophysical Hazards.  The student derives three testable hypotheses that stem directly from recognised demographic theories, demonstrating that the question supports hypothesis formation.  Minor wording slips (San Pablo vs San Rafael) do not detract from the essentially sound formulation.

Bar charts, line graph, pie charts and tables are appropriate to the data type and help the reader follow the argument.  However, axis labels, units and titles are occasionally unclear and some annotations obscure data, so the âmost effectiveâ techniques standard is not yet reached.

Convenience sampling, sample size (n = 240) and multilingual questionnaires show correct implementation, yet the convenience strategy is not critically addressed and some blank entries remain in the survey log.  Techniques are usable but data reliability is only moderate, fitting the middle band.

The student combines descriptive statistics (frequency graphs) with Spearmanâs rank correlations, showing correct calculation and basic interpretation.  No confidence testing or significance levels are given, so analysis is appropriate but not âcomprehensiveâ, meriting the top of the 3â4 band.

Trends (positive correlations) and anomalies are identified and related back to each hypothesis and the fieldwork question, but links to geophysical-hazard theory are brief and largely descriptive, meeting the 3â4 band but closer to its lower end.

Both cantons (EscazÃº and San Rafael) are precisely identified; maps and site tables specify parks, streets and markets, demonstrating that primary data collection was feasible.  The geographic tectonic setting is described and linked to local risk, giving a clear sense of place.

The student outlines several relevant strengths (sample size, bilingual questionnaire) and weaknesses (convenience bias, timing, manual calculations) with brief explanations of why they matter, satisfying the middle band for depth.

The primary dataset (income, education, age, preparedness scores) is largely relevant to the hypotheses and generally sufficient, but gaps such as missing respondents in the site log and unreported non-response rates prevent it from being fully comprehensive.

A single concluding paragraph summarises findings and states which hypotheses are accepted or rejected, but support is largely narrative; no statistical thresholds or margin-of-error discussion is provided, so the conclusion is only âpartially supportedâ by the preceding analysis.

All three hypotheses are addressed, yet significance testing is absent and partial data (e.g., missing blank responses) are ignored, leaving some gaps in evidence.  The analysis is therefore reasonably thorough but not complete.

Methods (questionnaire structure, coding scheme, Spearmanâs rank) are described and clearly related to each hypothesis, but explanations remain brief and theoretical justification for choosing each test is limited; therefore they are âdescribed with clear relevanceâ rather than comprehensively explained.

Most figures are numbered and captioned, but several graphs lack units on axes and one map omits a north arrow; colour keys are sometimes indistinct.  These are âoccasional errorsâ, placing the work in the 1â2 band with the higher mark awarded for generally acceptable practice.

Improvement suggestions (different survey times, ensure honesty, use software for calculations) are relevant and their potential impact is mentioned, but they are not elaborated in detail, aligning with the 2-mark descriptor.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly geographical and directly linked to seismic preparedness, effectively setting the scope for hypothesis formation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Split lines in the question disrupt readability; consolidate into a single paragraph to present a seamless research question.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Location name inconsistency: âSan Pabloâ is used instead of âSan Rafael,â creating confusion. Ensure consistent toponymy.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The link to the IB syllabus is explicit and relevant, demonstrating a clear theoretical connection with Option D: Geophysical Hazards.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The geographic context paragraph is largely descriptive. Include local demographic statistics or socioeconomic data to strengthen context specificity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Opening statement is vague. Replace âThe community of the countryâ¦â with a precise objective describing specific sites and populations.
Figure 1 lacks a north arrow and scale bar, reducing spatial intelligence. Add these conventions to improve map clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The context of EscazÃº vs San Rafael is asserted by educational level without citing data or source. Provide specific indicators with references.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 3 omits a scale bar; this compromises spatial accuracy. Include both scale and north arrow for consistent mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The description of EscazÃº site locations lacks precise coordinates or landmarks. Add latitude/longitude or more exact descriptors for reproducibility.
Figure 4âs map is useful but has no legend for demographic overlays. Annotate symbols or colours to clarify sampling areas.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 1 is logically stated, but the brief rationale lacks linkage to demographic theory. Integrate theoretical justification for economic impact.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 2 presents a locator map but omits scale and north arrow; follow mapping conventions (legend, orientation) to enhance usability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypotheses 2 and 3 explanations are verbose and repetitive. Condense and focus on the unique theoretical premise for each demographic factor.
Site selection rationale uses HDI rankings, which is strong. It clearly justifies why cantons were chosen based on development differences.
Questionnaire conversion to numerical scores is described inconsistently (0â2 vs 1â3). Standardize and clearly justify the scoring rubric.
The benefits section lists generic advantages of questionnaires without linking to research question validity. Add critical reflection on reliability and bias.
The questionnaire table is overcrowded and columns are misaligned. Simplify layout and ensure each variable occupies its own clear column.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure 7 annotations overlap text, reducing clarity. Redraw annotations or use numbering linked to a legend to enhance readability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figures 12 and 13 effectively contrast age distributions, facilitating visual comparison.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Age correlation test uses n=239 despite total sample of 240. Confirm exclusion criteria or correct denominator for accuracy.
Conclusion restates hypotheses and results but lacks explicit mention of confidence or marginâofâerror; strengthen support by referencing statistical thresholds.
Identifies sample size as a strength, explicitly linking to reliability. Consider also reflecting on representativeness of demographic subgroups.
The survey log table has several blank rows (e.g., Student 19) and inconsistencies in place names. Clean and verify entries to ensure data integrity.
The statement âSan Rafael was done on <math>4.</math>â is incomplete and misâformatted. Correct the math tag and clarify the number of days.
Convenience sampling is correctly implemented but unaddressed bias may arise. Outline potential sampling bias and mitigation strategies.
Point allocation description conflicts with earlier scheme. Ensure consistency in coding (e.g., 0â2 vs 1â3) and document the final rubric.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The analysis introduction for Hypothesis 1 does not explain why income is expected to correlate with preparedness. Link back explicitly to theory.
Figure 8âs axes lack units and title clarity. Label both axes with variable names, units, and data source to improve presentation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The Spearmanâs rank formula is correctly shown with clear notation, demonstrating sound statistical technique.
Correlation coefficients are reported without significance levels or p-values. Include statistical significance to support analytical conclusions.
The interpretation of moderate correlations for both cantons is sound, but lacks linkage to seismic vulnerability theory.
Introduce Hypothesis 2 by specifying the expected direction of relationship to reinforce analytic clarity.
Education table lacks a caption and overly compressed layout. Add a clear title and adjust cell padding to improve readability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Graphical comparison of education levels uses similar colours without contrast. Use distinct hues or patterns to differentiate series.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Spearmanâs rank outputs are presented but degrees of freedom and sample size context are missing. Include these details for completeness.
Interpretation of EscazÃºâs weak correlation is accurate but could be deepened by exploring potential outliers or sampling artifacts.
Weakness section identifies timing and honesty issues. Enhance by suggesting alternative survey windows with rationale for improved cooperation.
